ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken soup' class (FOODON_03311069); no specific 'cream of chicken soup' subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers chicken soup generically; no cream-of-chicken-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for cream of chicken soup identified in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of cream of chicken soup weighing approximately 250 g, as typically served as a starter in an Indian-influenced restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Soup, cream of chicken, canned, condensed (SR Legacy ID 06016) and Soup, cream of chicken, prepared with equal volume water (SR Legacy ID 06017)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for freshly prepared Indian-restaurant cream of chicken soup in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases. Values adapted from USDA SR Legacy cream-of-chicken soup entries (condensed, prepared with water) and cross-checked against USDA Foundation Foods chicken broth and heavy cream entries. Input record values (energy 128 kcal, protein 7.2 g, fat 9.6 g, carbohydrate 3.2 g) are consistent with a freshly prepared, higher-protein restaurant version and have been retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are sourced from USDA SR Legacy and are plausible for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for freshly prepared cream of chicken soup exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low category) based on the analogous entry for canned cream of tomato soup (GI ~38–52, University of Sydney GI Database) and the low net carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) of this preparation. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (50 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.0 g, classified as low GL. Estimation is conservative and defensible given the cream and protein content which further attenuates glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request low-sodium broth preparation, as the 300 mg/100 g sodium content (750 mg per standard 250 g bowl) approaches 33% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single serving, and the negligible potassium and fibre content provides no meaningful offset to the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic load (GL 4 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cream of chicken soup is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g), low glycemic load (GL ≈ 4 per 250 g bowl), and high protein (7.2 g/100 g) which slows gastric emptying; however, the saturated fat content (5.6 g/100 g) should be monitored as chronic high saturated fat intake worsens insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.6 g/100 g and sodium 300 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this soup sparingly, as the saturated fat content (5.6 g/100 g, contributing 14 g per 250 g bowl) exceeds the AHA recommended limit of 13 g saturated fat per day in a single serving, and the sodium content (750 mg per bowl) contributes substantially to daily cardiovascular risk load; substituting heavy cream with low-fat milk and using unsalted low-sodium broth would significantly improve the cardiovascular profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.6 g/100 g at pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit intake of this soup as the saturated fat content (5.6 g/100 g from butter and heavy cream) is at the threshold associated with upregulation of TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and the absence of omega-3 fatty acids, meaningful antioxidants, or polyphenols provides no anti-inflammatory counterbalance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cream of Chicken Soup is a rich, velvety soup made from tender chicken pieces simmered in a seasoned chicken broth and thickened with heavy cream and a small quantity of cornflour. In the Singapore Indian-restaurant context, it is typically served as a starter course and draws on both Western cream-soup technique and South Asian spicing traditions. The texture is smooth and creamy with soft chicken pieces, and the flavour is mild, savoury, and slightly buttery. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 128 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 9.6 g total fat (5.6 g saturated), 3.2 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g sugar, 0.2 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. A standard 250 g bowl delivers approximately 320 kcal and 750 mg sodium. The glycemic load per serving is low (approximately 4), making it suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes when consumed in moderation. However, the elevated saturated fat and sodium content make it a moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The soup is halal-certified in most Singapore Indian restaurant settings. It is commonly paired with bread rolls or crackers as a starter before a main course of curry or biryani.

confidence_reason: No exact match for freshly prepared Indian-restaurant cream of chicken soup found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values from the input record are consistent with USDA SR Legacy cream-of-chicken soup entries and have been retained. Micronutrients cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cream of tomato soup as the closest available reference. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match and restaurant-preparation variability.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for freshly prepared Indian-restaurant cream of chicken soup are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024 for this specific preparation. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.2 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with trace dairy-derived trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) present in butter and heavy cream; this is a plausible and defensible value. All core macros (protein_g 7.2, fat_g 9.6, carbohydrate_g 3.2) are non-zero and nutritionally justified for a cream-based chicken soup. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was evaluated but does not constitute an approved primary nutrient source for this dish; USDA FoodData Central was used as the approved reference instead. Ethnic tags in the input ('Butter Chicken', 'Curry', 'Biryani') are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded from the output. GI estimated at 50 using analogous cream-of-tomato soup data from the University of Sydney GI Database; actual GI measurement for this specific preparation is not available.
